coredata.dc:title: Inhibition behavior of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on X80 carbon steel in acidic solution
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Fanhong
Zhou, Guanggang
Zhu, Jianwei
Zhang, Xiao
Shi, Yanyan
Lu, Guiwu
Liu, Zilong
coredata.dc:description: In order to restrain the corrosion issue in the picking process of metal materials, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with a size of 2 ~ 4 nm have been fabricated via hydrothermal synthesis with citric acid and thiourea as precursors. The corrosion inhibition behavior of NCDs on X80 carbon steel were investigated in 0.1 M HCl solutions with electrochemical experiments, the weight loss method, and surface morphology analysis. The Tafel curves showed that the inhibition efficiency reached as high as 92.98% at the NCDs concentration of 10 mg·L−1, indicating that NCDs had significant anti–corrosion capacity for X80 carbon steel. Impedance spectroscopy data revealed that the diameter of the capacitive reactance increased with increasing the NCDs concentration. More corrosion inhibitor molecules can be adsorbed on the metal surface instead of water molecules, which enhanced corrosion resistance of carbon steel. Weight loss experiment and surface morphology analysis strongly supported the results of electrochemical experiments. In addition, the NCDs adsorption at X80 surface followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which was mainly dominated by physical adsorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCDs
Corrosion inhibitor
Electrochemical
Adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis process of NCDs.
Characterization of NCDs: (a) FTIR spectrum, (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (c) XPS spectrum, (d) XPS–C 1 s, (e) XPS–N 1 s,and (f) XPS–O 1 s.
(a) AFM image of NCDs and (b) its height profiles.
Tafel data of X80 carbon steel in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCDs.

EIS spectra of X80 carbon steel in 0.1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCDs (a) Nyquist, (b) Bode impedance modulus, (c) Bode phase angle, (d) the equivalent circuit fitting diagram of EIS results.
SEM images for the polished X80 carbon steel (a) before and (b) after 48 h of immersion in 0.1 M HCl solution in the absence and (c) the presence of 1 mg/L NCDs and (d)the presence of 10 mg/L NCDs.
Four adsorption isotherms of X80 steel surface in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCDs (a) Langmuir, (b) Freundlich, (c) Temkin, (d) Frumkin.
Weight loss experiment parameters of X80 carbon steel in the absence and presence of different concentrations of NCDs.
Parameters related to the Tafel curves.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from the EIS curves.

head.ce:title: Inhibition behavior of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on X80 carbon steel in acidic solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Optical Detection Technology for Oil and Gas, College of Science, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Optical Detection Technology for Oil and Gas

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Optical Detection Technology for Oil and Gas, College of Science, China University of Petroleum Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Beijing Key Laboratory of Optical Detection Technology for Oil and Gas, College of Science, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China (G. Lu and Z. Liu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Optical Detection Technology for Oil and Gas

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally as the first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to restrain the corrosion issue in the picking process of metal materials, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with a size of 2 ~ 4 nm have been fabricated via hydrothermal synthesis with citric acid and thiourea as precursors. The corrosion inhibition behavior of NCDs on X80 carbon steel were investigated in 0.1 M HCl solutions with electrochemical experiments, the weight loss method, and surface morphology analysis. The Tafel curves showed that the inhibition efficiency reached as high as 92.98% at the NCDs concentration of 10 mg·L−1, indicating that NCDs had significant anti–corrosion capacity for X80 carbon steel. Impedance spectroscopy data revealed that the diameter of the capacitive reactance increased with increasing the NCDs concentration. More corrosion inhibitor molecules can be adsorbed on the metal surface instead of water molecules, which enhanced corrosion resistance of carbon steel. Weight loss experiment and surface morphology analysis strongly supported the results of electrochemical experiments. In addition, the NCDs adsorption at X80 surface followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which was mainly dominated by physical adsorption.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, X80 carbon steel have been extensively used in engineering fields, such as oil and gas transportation, chemical processing, military and maritime areas, and cooling water systems due to their great mechanical properties, comprehensive mechanical properties, high hardness and wear resistance, low price and other properties [1–5]. However, after a long period of operation, the inner surface of carbon steel is prone to foul and corrosion, which would damage the mechanical strength, structure or failure of the equipment, resulting in great economic losses and even serious safety of life. The most common method is to clean the inner surface of the equipment with a pickling solution, among which HCl is one of the most commonly used pickling solutions, because it can shorten the pickling time and achieve better surface quality [6]. Although pickling solution can effectively eliminate the oxidation corrosion, it also causes a certain extent of corrosion to the substrate of the equipment. According to statistics, the annual global cost of corrosion is $250 million, and the cost is expected to grow as technology advances [7]. In order to reduce the corrosion of metal materials, many anti-corrosion methods have been used to explore the corrosion of metal.

It is an effective method to add corrosion inhibitor into pickling solution, which has the advantages of low cost, less dosage, strong practicability, and strong inhibition performance [8–10]. Chromates, mercury salts and phosphorus compounds are the earliest inorganic corrosion inhibitors [11–12]. With the development of corrosion inhibitors, organic corrosion inhibitors, including some heteroatoms (N, O, S, and P), unsaturated bonds or polar functional groups, and various aromatic ring plane conjugated systems, have been widely used [13–19]. These heteroatoms or unsaturated groups, by acting as adsorption centers, can interact with the unoccupied orbitals of the transition metal to form coordination bonds. Thus, corrosion inhibitor molecules can be stably adsorbed on metal surfaces to block corrosive media. Despite the fact that these organic compounds have excellent corrosion inhibition performance, they also have certain shortcomings, such as high toxicity, complex synthesis route, high cost, and difficulty in biodegradation [20–22]. Therefore, it is essential to consider significant protective effects, low cost, easy availability and environmental protection when choosing corrosion inhibitors [23–24].

Carbon dots (CDs) are novel fluorescent nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm [25]. Their many advantages, such as low toxicity [26], easy surface modification [27], good water solubility [28], a wide range of carbon sources, and simple preparation method [29], have attracted increasing attention in recent years. For example, Cui et al. [30] prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) through hydrothermal synthesis with the antibiotic salicylic acid as the raw material and applied it in corrosion research for the first time. Their results showed that the inhibition efficiency of NCDs was 90.9% at the concentration of 10 mg·L−1. Later, Cui et al. [31] prepared NCDs with p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials. Their results demonstrated that with the increase in NCDs concentration, the corrosion current density decreased significantly, and the corrosion inhibition performance was enhanced. Ye et al. [32] prepared a sort of NCDs by the reaction between citric acid and N-(3-aminopropyl) imidazole, and found that inhibitory efficiency of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl reached 93.4% at the concentration of 200 mg·L−1. Cen et al. [33] synthesized a green corrosion inhibitor with p-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as raw materials. Their results illustrated that the CDs could be adsorbed on the surface of carbon steel to isolate corrosive media. Yang et al. [25] synthesized a IM-CDs with imidazole ionic liquid and citric acid as raw materials, which was used to study the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in HCl and NaOH solutions. The results showed that the inhibition efficiency of the CDs on Q235 carbon steel was more than 80% at 200 mg·L−1, showing good inhibition performance.

In this work, citric acid and thiourea were used as precursors to obtain efficient and green NCDs. The NCDs was applied to study the corrosion behavior of carbon steel in 0.1 M HCl solution. To the best of our knowledge, the preparation of NCDs with citric acid and thiourea as corrosion inhibitors have not been previously reported. The fabrication of NCDs was prepared with citric acid and thiourea as the raw materials for the following reasons: (1) The autoclave used in the hydrothermal synthesis method was a completely enclosed instrument, which would not cause harm to the human body and the environment during the reaction, and the method was simple and easy to operate; (2) Use citric acid as the carbon source, thiourea as the nitrogen source, and ethanol as the solvent. These substances are commonly used reagents and cost-effective; (3) The reaction formula contains N and S elements, which act as dopants and easily modify the surfaces of CDs. The corrosion inhibition effect of NCDs on carbon steel was quantitatively evaluated characterized by a weightlessness experiment, polarization curve analysis, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Meanwhile, the structure of NCDs and the surface morphology of the corrosion products were characterized through the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS), Atom Force Microscopy (AFM), X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Atomic Force Microscope(AFM).

Citric acid (AR, 99.7%) was acquired from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd, Thiourea (CP, 99.5%) was procured from West Asia Chemical Technology (Shandong) Co., Ltd, Ethanol (AR, 99.7%) and HCl (37 wt%) were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Co., Ltd. The corrosive solution used in the experiment was prepared by diluting HCl with deionized water. X80 carbon steel was purchased from a local supplier, with the elementary composition (wt%): P 0.011, Si 0.28, Ni 0.03, Mn 1.83, C 0.063, S 0.0006, Cr 0.03, Ti 0.016, V 0.059, Mo 0.22, and Fe balance. Before the experiment, the carbon steel was cut into samples with sizes of 20 mm × 20 mm × 6 mm; polished with 400#, 800#, and 1200# grit sandpaper, and finally rinsed with ethanol, and deionized water, dried and weighted.

The synthesis of NCDs in this work followed the similar step reported by Cen et al [33], as shown in Fig. 1 . 0.6 g of citric acid and 0.2 g of thiourea were weighed out and ultrasonically dissolved in 37 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a tetrafluoroethylene autoclave. After heating at 200 °C in an oven for 16 h and cooling down to room temperature naturally, a black and transparent product was obtained. Then, a 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used to filter large particle products. Subsequently, the crude product was purified with a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off ~ 3.0 kDa) for 24 h to remove the unreactive molecules. Finally, the solution was transferred to a vacuum drying oven at 70 °C for 24 h to obtain NCDs. The structure of NCDs was investigated by using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermofei Nicolet iS5) and UV–Vis spectrum apparatus (Shanghai Maple Instruments Co., Ltd.). XPS (Thermo Fisher Nexsa) and EDS (FEI Quanta200F) were performed to identify the chemical composition and bonding status of the NCDs. SEM (FEI Quanta200F) was performed to observe the morphology of the X80 carbon steel. AFM (5500 AFM) was used to obtain the particle size and distribution of NCDs.

Firstly, five X80 carbon steel samples with dimensions of 20 mm × 20 mm × 6 mm were placed in a constant temperature thermostat for 6 h. Then, the initial weight W 0 of each sample was measured three times, and the average value was taken. Samples were immersed in 0.1 M HCl solution in the absence and presence of various concentrations of NCDs for 48 h. Then, all the samples were taken out from the solutions, rinsed with ethanol and deionized water to remove the corrosion products, and dried with cold air. Subsequently, the weight W 1 of each sample was measured three times for calculations. The corrosion rate (υ corr) was acquired through the following equation [34]:
(1)
υ corr
=
W 0
-
W 1
St

Where W 1 and W 0 were the qualities of the sample after corrosion and before corrosion, respectively. S was the area of the carbon steel sample exposed to the corrosive medium (cm2), and t was the immersion time (h).

All electrochemical measurements were conducted in a CHI760 electrochemical station (produced by Shanghai Chenhua) with a traditional three-electrode system, saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and platinum foil were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively. X80 carbon steel with the exposure area of 1 cm2 was the working electrode. All experiments were carried out at room temperature. Before each measurement, the working electrode was immersed in a corrosive solution for 30 min until the open circuit potential reached a stable state. Then, the experimental measurement was started. EIS was performed in a frequency range of 100 kHz–10 mHz at the OCP with 10 mV of disturbance signal. The EIS data were fitted and analyzed with ZSimDemo software. Finally, the Tafel polarization test was carried out at ± 250 mV versus the OCP at 0.5 mV·s−1. The same experiment was performed for at least three times to ensure the reliability.

Table 1 presented the average weight loss (ΔW) and υ corr of X80 carbon steel in various NCDs conditions at 298 K. After 48 h immersion. It was found that adding a small amount of NCDs could significantly reduce the corrosion rate of X80 carbon steel. Furthermore, the corrosion rate decreased rapidly with the increase in the NCDs concentration. For example, the corrosion rate of X80 carbon steel treated with 1 mg·L−1 NCDs was 80% lower than that of the blank sample. The corrosion rate of X80 carbon steel treated with 10 mg·L−1 NCDs was 90% lower than that of the blank sample. These results showed that the synthesized NCDs had excellent corrosion inhibition performance.

Fig. 2
. showed the FTIR, UV–vis, and XPS spectra of the NCDs. As seen in FTIR spectrum, a series of sharp absorption peaks located at 3225, 2984, 1721, and 1026 cm−1, corresponding to O–H, C–H, C = O, and C–O stretching vibrations [34], respectively. The weak absorption peaks observed at 3480, 2080, and 1374 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H, N = C = O, and C–N [33,35], respectively. The appearance of N = C = O peak suggested that the thiourea was successfully reacted with the citric acid. The UV–vis spectrum exhibited, a strong absorption peak was observed at 340 nm that might be related to the transfer of Π–Π* electrons [25]. A detailed analysis on the XPS spectrum was performed to study the chemical composition of NCDs. The XPS spectrum mainly contained C, O, and N elements (Fig. 2c), The XPS spectra of each element was fitted (Fig. 2d, 2e, and 2f) to investigate the existence of C, O, and N elements. The C1s spectra displayed four peaks at 284.6 285.1, 286.3, and 287.9 eV that corresponded to C–C, C–N, C–O and O = C–N bonds [34,36], respectively. The three peaks fitted in the N1s spectrum at 398.9, 399.9 and 400.9 eV were attributed to N–H, N–C and N = C bonds, respectively [37]. The two peaks fitted in the O 1 s spectrum at 531.8 and 533.0 eV, corresponded to C = O and C–O bonds [38], respectively. These results indicated that nitrogen was successfully doped into the CDs. As could be roughly inferred from the characterization results of FTIR and XPS, the NCDs had a heterocyclic ring structure that contained groups, such as –OH, N = C, N–C = O, N–C, and –CO–. The AFM image was used to examine the particle size and distribution of as-prepared NCDs. As shown in Fig. 3 , the average sizes of NCDs were about 2 ~ 4 nm, and most NCDs particles were monodispersed, whereas there were some aggregated, implying a good distribution.

The Tafel curves and EIS of X80 carbon steel in corrosion solutions with different concentrations were obtained. The Tafel curves were used to determine corrosion rates on the basis of experimental polarization curves [39]. This determination technique involved extrapolating the current in the anode and cathode Tafel regions to the corrosion potential (E corr). Fig. 4 provided the polarization curves of X80 carbon steel that had been immersed for 48 h in solutions containing different concentrations of NCDs. It could be seen that compared with E corr and corrosion current density (i corr) of the solution without NCDs, the corrosion potential of the solution with different concentrations of NCDs moved toward a negative direction. However, during the whole experiment, the change area of corrosion potential was less than 85 mV. The results showed that the NCDs was a mixed-type inhibitor [2,40,41] that could inhibit the corrosion of the anode and cathode at the same time.

By calculating the Tafel curves, the following parameters were obtained in Table 2 , E corr, i corr, the slope of the Tafel anode curve (β a), the slope of the Tafel cathode curve (β c), surface coverage (θ), and corrosion inhibition efficiency (ŋ). θ and ŋ values could be calculated as follows [25]:
(2)
θ =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
i
corr
0
(3)
IE =
i
corr
0
-
i
corr
i
corr
0
× 100
Where i0 corr and icorr represented the current densities in the absence or presence of NCDs solutions with different concentrations, respectively.

Obviously, i corr value was reduced by one order of magnitude after the addition of NCDs. The values of θ and ŋ increase with increasing concentration, and corrosion efficiency reached as high as 92.98% at 10 mg·L−1. The results showed that the addition of NCDs significantly inhibited the corrosion of X80 carbon steel. Compared with previously reported inhibitors [42–44], NCDs showed a higher corrosion inhibition efficiency. Meanwhile, the variation range of β a was greater than that of β c. It indicated that the corrosion inhibitor inhibited the cathode reaction more obviously, which was attributed to the adsorption of NCDs on the metal surface. These results were in good agreement with our weightlessness measurements (Table 1).

EIS could be used to derive further the corrosion inhibition behavior of NCDs on X80 carbon steel. Fig. 5 showed the Nyquist and Bode diagrams of carbon steel in different test solutions. Fig. 5a revealed that the Nyquist curves in different concentration were similar, and showed that there was a large reactance arc at high frequency (HF) and a small inductive arc at low frequency (LF), indicating that the additions of NCDs did not change the mechanism of the electrochemical reaction [45,46]. HF capacitor circuits were related to the charge transfer or resistance of corrosion products/adsorbed inhibitor films, and the LF inductive were related to the relaxation of the adsorbed intermediate that controls the anodic process [30,47]. In addition, with the increase in the concentration of the corrosion inhibitor, the diameter of the capacitive reactance arc increased, because more corrosion inhibitor molecules were adsorbed on the metal surface instead of water molecules, resulting in the increase of the thickness of the adsorption films and the enhanced inhibition of carbon steel corrosion.

Fig. 5b showed the Bode impedance modulus diagram. The lowest frequency impedance values (|Z|f = 0.01 Hz) could be used to evaluate the corrosion resistance of carbon steel in a medium [48,49]. In general, a high impedance modulus at the lowest frequency indicated the great corrosion inhibition performance of samples [30]. As could be seen from the figure, the impedance modulus at the lowest frequency increased with the increase in concentration, and at C = 10 mg·L−1, the corresponding impedance modulus at the low frequency was the highest. These results showed that the good adsorption performance of NCDs improved the surface coverage of NCDs on carbon steel, and that the NCDs polymer film had a good protective effect on carbon steel.

Fig. 5c showed two time constants in the Bode phase angle diagram. The time constant of the middle frequency and HF was related to the response of the film, and the time constant of the LF was related to charge transfer [50]. An appropriate equivalent circuit model was proposed for the EIS results of all samples Fig. 5d to understand corrosion dynamics further. In the equivalent circuit [3], the parameters R s and R p represent solution resistance and polarization resistance, respectively; and L was the inductance and R L was its corresponding resistance; the constant phase component CPE was an ideal capacitor, where the CPE contained a double-layer capacitance (C1) and phenomenological coefficient (n). A constant phase component representing a displacement from an ideal capacitor was used instead of the capacitance. The impedance of a phase component was defined as [3]:
(4)
Z
CPE
=
[
C 1
( j ω )
n
]
- 1

where C1 was capacitance, j was the current, ω was the frequency, −1 ≤ n ≤ 1. The value of n seemed to be associated with the non-uniform distribution of current as a result of roughness and surface defects. The n value of a CPE indicated: n = 1, a capacitance; n = 0.5, a Warburg impedance; n = 0, a resistance and n = − 1, an inductance. In the present study, n was consistently maintained near 0.8, due to the deviation from ideal dielectric properties.

The corresponding electrochemical parameters were listed in Table 3
. The inductance L in the EIS results indicated that the dissolution of X80 carbon steel still occurred through charge transfer in the presence of NCDs [30,51]. An inspection of the results in the Table 3 revealed that, with the increase in concentration, the value of R s basically remained unchanged, that of R P decreased, and that of CPE decreased. These results indicated that NCDs exerted a good anti − corrosion effect on carbon steel exposed to 0.1 M HCl solution.

The corrosion morphology was presented in Fig. 6
. Fig. 6a showed that the polished X80 carbon steel had some polished scratches but was relatively smooth. Fig. 6b showed the X80 carbon steel soaked in 0.1 M HCl solution. The surface of the carbon steel was rough and uneven in many places, indicating that the carbon steel was seriously corroded in acidic solution. However, the corrosion of X80 carbon steel was significantly inhibited by the addition of 1 mg·L−1 NCDs Fig. 6c and 10 mg·L−1 NCDs Fig. 6d. Compared with the carbon steel soaked in 0.1 M HCl solution, the treated carbon steel samples showed diminished uneven pits. This change was highly evident in Fig. 6d. Nevertheless, scratches still remained.

The elemental composition of the surface of X80 carbon steel was analyzed ( Fig. S1 ). Carbon steel consisted mainly of elemental Fe, C, and O. However, even the blank sample contained a trace amount of Cl. Fig. S1
(a) likely because of the presence of the Cl element in the atmospheric environment. The trace elemental Cl detected in Fig. S1
(b–d) was formed by the residual chloride of Fe after the reaction of carbon steel with 0.1 M HCl. In other words, given that the concentration of HCl was low, the content of elemental Cl was also low. Fig. S1 showed that with the increase in NCDs concentration, the content of Fe decreased, whereas the content of O and C tended to increase because NCDs adsorbed to sites that were rich in these elements, resulting in the increase in C and O elements. Therefore, consistent with the results of FTIR and XPS, NCDs could be considered to adsorb on the surfaces of carbon steel through the active sites containing these elements [40].

The interaction and inhibition mechanism between the corrosion inhibitor and metal surface could be analyzed by understanding the adsorption isotherm curves between the surface of carbon steel and the corrosion inhibitor. Among the many adsorption isotherms, the ones that were commonly used include the Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, and Frumkin isotherms. The corresponding fitting results were shown in Fig. 7. Within the experimental data range, the Langmuir curve (a) had the best fitting effect among the four curves and the linear correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9991. Therefore, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm was the most suitable for the inhibition effect curve of the change in NCDs concentration. The linear fitting relationship could be expressed as [48]:
(5)
C
inh
θ
=
1
K
ads
+
C
inh

where θ and Cinh were the surface coverage rate and inhibitor concentration, respectively, and K ads was the adsorption equilibrium constant. Generally speaking, large K ads values were indicative of the strong adsorption of NCDs on the metal surface [52]. The value of K ads in this work was 132.4 L·g−1.

The interaction energy between the NCDs and the surface of carbon steel could be analyzed on the basis of the standard adsorption free energy (
Δ
G
ads
0
), which could be obtained by using Eq. (5) [12]:
(6)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - R T I n
( 1000
K
ads
)
where T and R represented the absolute temperature (298 K) and the molar gas constant (8.314 J mol−1·K−1). In Eq. (5), the concentration of water was 1000 g·L−1 because the unit of K ads was L·g−1.

Physical adsorption and chemical adsorption were the common adsorption processes of corrosion inhibitors on metal surfaces and were closely related to the value of
Δ
G
ads
0
[25].
Δ
G
ads
0
was a vital parameter for estimating the spontaneity of a process. If
Δ
G
ads
0
was negative, the adsorption process occured spontaneously at a given temperature [53]. Actually, when the absolute value of
Δ
G
ads
0
was less than or equal to 20 kJ·mol−1, the main adsorption form of the corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface was physical adsorption, which was caused by van der Waals forces and electrostatic attraction. When the absolute value of
Δ
G
ads
0
was equal to or greater than 40 kJ·mol−1, chemical adsorption mainly occured. The adsorption of the corrosion inhibitor on the metal surface was caused by the transfer or sharing of electrons to form a covalent bond. The absolute value of physicochemical adsorption was between 20 and 40 kJ·mol−1 [54,55]. Considering that
Δ
G
ads
0
= –29.21 kJ·mol−1, in this work, the NCDs was adsorbed on the metal surface through physical and chemical interactions, of which physical adsorption played a dominant role. In other words, the adsorption film formed through physical adsorption to block the active site on the metal surface to protect the metal from corrosion.

In this work, we successfully fabricated NCDs using citric acid and thiourea as precursors by a facile solvothermal method. The corrosion inhibition mechanism of NCDs on X80 carbon steel in 0.1 M HCl solution was comprehensively investigated. The weight loss experiments demonstrated the corrosion rate of X80 carbon steel was 90% lower in the presence of 10 mg·L−1 NCDs than that of the blank experiment. Surface characterizations showed that the average size of prepared NCDs was 2 ~ 4 nm, having a heterocyclic ring structure that contained groups, such as –OH, N = C, N–C = O, N–C, and –CO–. Through electrochemical measurements, the impedance modulus of carbon steel in the HCl solution increased from 24 to 290 Ω·cm2, when the NCDs concentration of was increased from 0 to 10 mg·L−1. The increase in the diameter of the impedance loop indicated that NCDs were conductive to the diffusion of ions, and the appropriate concentration of the corrosion inhibitor can be obtained. The Tafel results showed that the optimal inhibition efficiency of NCDs was 92.98% at the NCDs concentration of 10 mg·L−1. The consistency between the observation on surface morphology and the results of the electrochemical and weightlessness experiments further indicated that NCDs showed high inhibition efficiency for X80 carbon steel in 0.1 M HCl solution. According to Langmuir's calculation results, the value was −29.21 kJ mol−1, indicating that the inhibition of the prepared NCDs followed the physical and chemical adsorption mechanism, but was mainly dominated by physical adsorption.

Fanhong Niu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft. Guanggang Zhou: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Jianwei Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiao Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Yanyan Shi: Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration. Guiwu Lu: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Zilong Liu: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Shanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Project (No. 20181101013), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51372140), Shandong University Young Scholars Program (No. 2016WLJH27), and the Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No. 2462020BJRC007, 2462020YXZZ003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.117171.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Corrosion resistance of carbon steel in weak acid solutions in the presence of L-histidine as corrosion inhibitor
Corros. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Bobina, A. Kellenberger, J.-P. Millet, C. Muntean, N. Vaszilcsin, Corrosion resistance of carbon steel in weak acid solutions in the presence of L-histidine as corrosion inhibitor, Corros. Sci. 69 (2013) , 389-395.
